nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments with moderate water movement and indirect to moderate light

decorPreferences: stable rubble or frag plugs for attachment

diet.notes: Primarily nourished by symbiotic zooxanthellae in the presence of adequate lighting. Supplemental feeding with zooplankton (rotifers, phytoplankton) or fine particulate filter-feeder foods has been reported to benefit coloration and growth, though the coral generally does not require target feeding in a well-lit, established reef tank.

goodWith: non-aggressive soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles (hammer, torch, frogspawn)
allelopathic corals placed in direct contact

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by prilfish, 4000x3000. Whole arborescent tree-like colony under blue reef lighting; vivid electric-blue branching form with feathery non-pulsing polyps — the classic 'Blue Tree Coral' look hobbyists buy. Sharp, well-framed.
Photo by prilfish, 4000x3000. Close view of the same coral showing individual blue octocoral polyps spread along branching stems; good diagnostic detail, slightly tighter crop so kept as secondary.

summary: Cespitularia is a branching, tree-like soft coral in the family Xeniidae, commonly sold as Blue Tree Coral or Blue Xenia. Though related to the pulsing Xenia corals, its polyps do not pulse. Care is considered intermediate, and the coral is sensitive to sudden swings in alkalinity, temperature, and salinity.
careNotes:
Cespitularia belongs to the family Xeniidae — the same family as the pulse corals (Xenia spp.) — but unlike Xenia, its polyps do NOT pulse rhythmically. The trade name "Blue Xenia" is a misnomer that arose from this shared family membership and a similar blue coloration. In reality, true Xenia polyps are larger and more densely bundled at the stalk tip, while Cespitularia polyps are smaller and spread along branching stems in a tree-like (arborescent) arrangement. That branching, tree-like form is exactly what the trade name "Blue Tree Coral" refers to — it has no relationship to tree corals belonging to other families.

Several species are sold under the Cespitularia name in the aquarium trade, including C. sp. "Vargas", C. sp. "Starlight", and specimens morphologically identified as C. erecta. Definitive species-level identification would require genetic testing; in practice, the genus designation is what the trade uses consistently across all of these forms.

Care considerations:
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally recommended, roughly 100–200 PAR. Intense high-par conditions tend to wash out color; lower light areas toward the middle of the tank are often preferred. Color can shift dramatically (from blue/purple to lighter blue) depending on lighting spectrum and intensity.
• Flow: Moderate flow is generally advised, sufficient to cause polyps to sway gently. Random or turbulent flow is preferred over laminar, which can abrade tissue. Inadequate flow allows detritus to accumulate at the stalk base and can lead to bacterial base rot.
• Acclimation: New specimens tend to require a careful acclimation period. Starting in lower-flow, lower-light areas for the first 5–10 days and gradually moving to final placement has been reported to improve survival. Cespitularia can crash rapidly from alkalinity swings, sudden temperature changes, or shipping stress.
• Attachment: The stalk can be anchored in a rock crevice, glued to a frag plug or rubble, and will attach naturally within days to weeks.
• Parameter stability: Unlike some hardier soft corals, Cespitularia has been observed to decline when alkalinity, temperature, or salinity fluctuate even moderately.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting stalks) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Individual stalks re-attach readily to rubble or frag plugs and grow into new colonies. The coral also spreads naturally via stolons in the aquarium. Sexual reproduction occurs on wild reefs but is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Vargas Cespitularia Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature, salinity, pH, kH, calcium, magnesium) and PAR range 150–250. BRS gives temperature 76–80°F vs. LiveAquaria 72–78°F; averaged to 22–27°C. Calcium 380–450 ppm and magnesium 1200–1400 ppm confirmed from this source.

additional.name: TankFacts – Blue Cespitularia
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), kH (8–12 dKH), calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm). Confirmed moderate lighting and moderate flow.

additional.name: Frag Box Corals – Cespitularia Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed PAR range (low-medium, ~80–150), medium flow, and photosynthetic diet. Native range Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef. Care level listed as beginner on this source; intermediate adopted per user specification and crash risk documented across sources.

additional.name: ReefCentral – Cespitularia vs Blue Xenia thread
additional.notes: Used to document the Blue Xenia naming confusion: 'The Name Blue Xenia is just a common name that was used for Cespitularia.' Confirmed non-pulsing behavior and distinction in polyp arrangement vs. true Xenia.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Cespitularia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: Order Malacalcyonacea, Family Xeniidae, Genus Cespitularia (Milne Edwards & Haime, 1850). Not used for husbandry parameters.
